summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on his decade-long quest for the perfect ultraportable PC laptop, culminating in the Asus Zenbook Prime UX31A—the best PC laptop he's ever owned. Despite finally finding hardware that matches his 2003 dream of reasonable power with extreme portability, he realizes the landscape has fundamentally shifted. Tablets and phones now offer superior battery life, instant-on convenience, and true portability that even the best ultrabook can't match. He concludes with a bittersweet observation that just as the PC industry finally learned to copy Apple's designs successfully, the relevance of laptops themselves may have passed.
key_points: The Asus Zenbook Prime UX31A represents the culmination of a decade of ultraportable laptop evolution, offering a high-quality IPS display, solid build quality, and decent battery life for around $1,050
PC laptops still suffer from the same problems as a decade ago: bloatware, ugly branding stickers, and trackpads that never quite work as well as they should
Tablets and phones have fundamentally changed the calculus of portable computing with 10-hour battery life, instant-on operation, cellular connectivity, and one-handed usability
Windows 8 represents the biggest step forward since Windows 95, but even its improved wake times can't compete with the instant-on nature of mobile devices
The laptop is increasingly relegated to situations requiring extensive typing or large screen real estate—a shrinking use case
money_quotes: The PC industry isn't exactly known for its design leadership, and it can be downright schizophrenic at times.
The Achilles heel of all PC laptops, the trackpad, is about as good as it ever gets on PCs. Which is to say still not great.
Every time I leave the house – heck, every time I leave the room – I have to decide what kind of computer I'm going to take with me, if any.
There is no form factor more compact than the touchscreen tablet.
Just try opening a laptop on a crowded subway train or bus. Or with, say, 3 toddlers running around your house. I dare you.
My laptop is increasingly a device I only take when I know I'll need to do a lot of typing, and/or I'll need a lot of screen space to work.

I've waited 12 years for the PC industry to get its collective act together and, if nothing else, successfully copy Apple's laptop hardware designs. Now that they (mostly) have, I wonder: is it too late?
I can't shake the feeling that this might end up being the last PC laptop I ever own.
key_insight: By the time the PC industry finally produced the perfect ultraportable laptop, mobile devices had already redefined what portable computing means, rendering that achievement somewhat hollow.
